exercises.title: ROI Calculation
exercises.description: Calculate return on investment for computational chemistry.
exercises.task:
A pharmaceutical company is considering a $2M investment in computational chemistry infrastructure over 3 years.

Expected outcomes:
- Reduce compounds synthesized by 40% (was 500/year at $20K each)
- Identify 2 additional clinical candidates (each worth $50M in NPV)
- Avoid 1 failed Phase I trial ($30M saved)

Calculate:
(a) Annual savings from reduced synthesis
(b) Total value generated over 3 years
(c) ROI for the computational chemistry investment

solution.part_a: Synthesis savings: 500 × 0.40 × $20K = $4M per year. Over 3 years: $12M in synthesis savings alone.
solution.part_b: Total value: Synthesis savings: $12M. Additional candidates: 2 × $50M = $100M. Avoided trial: $30M. Total: $12M + $100M + $30M = $142M.
solution.part_c: ROI = (Value - Investment) / Investment = ($142M - $2M) / $2M = 70× return. Even if only half the projected value materializes, ROI = 35×, still excellent.

hints: NPV of candidates is already discounted

exercises.title: Build vs Buy Decision
exercises.description: Analyze whether to build internal capabilities or outsource.
exercises.task:
A mid-size biotech company needs quantum chemistry capabilities.

Option A - Build Internal Team:
- 3 computational chemists: $450K/year salary+benefits
- Software licenses: $200K/year
- Computing infrastructure: $300K initial + $50K/year
- Time to full productivity: 18 months

Option B - Outsource to CRO:
- Per-project cost: $150K average
- Expected 8 projects/year
- No infrastructure needed
- Immediate availability

Analyze:
(a) 3-year total cost of each option
(b) Which factors favor Option A?
(c) Which factors favor Option B?
(d) What's your recommendation for a 5-project/year scenario?

solution.part_a: Option A (3 years): Personnel: 3 × $450K × 3 = $4.05M. Software: $200K × 3 = $600K. Computing: $300K + ($50K × 3) = $450K. Total: $5.1M. Option B (3 years): 8 projects × $150K × 3 = $3.6M.
solution.part_b: Factors favoring Option A (internal): Long-term cost advantage if >10 projects/year. Proprietary knowledge stays in-house. Faster iteration and integration. Can respond quickly to needs. IP protection. Build institutional knowledge.
solution.part_c: Factors favoring Option B (outsource): Lower initial investment. Immediate availability. Flexibility to scale up/down. Access to diverse expertise. No recruitment challenges. Lower risk if projects decline.
solution.part_d: For 5 projects/year: Option B cost = 5 × $150K × 3 = $2.25M (vs $5.1M internal). Recommend Option B: Lower cost, lower risk, immediate availability. Consider internal team when project volume exceeds 10-12/year sustained.

exercises.title: Project Prioritization
exercises.task:
Your computational chemistry team has limited capacity (1000 CPU-hours/week) and must prioritize these requests:

Project A: Virtual screen of 10K compounds
- CPU: 500 hours
- Deadline: 2 weeks
- Value: Guide HTS, save $200K

Project B: FEP calculations for 20 compound pairs
- CPU: 800 hours
- Deadline: 1 week
- Value: Lead optimization, critical path

Project C: Mechanism study for patent
- CPU: 300 hours
- Deadline: 3 weeks
- Value: Strengthen IP, $5M patent value

Project D: ADMET predictions for 50 compounds
- CPU: 200 hours
- Deadline: 4 weeks
- Value: Preclinical support

How would you prioritize and schedule these projects?

solution.priority: Recommended priority: B > A > C > D
solution.rationale: Project B: Highest urgency (1 week deadline) and on critical path. Must complete first. Week 1: 800 hours. Project A: Important deadline (2 weeks) and good ROI. Week 2: 500 hours. Project C: High value but flexible timeline. Week 2-3: 300 hours (split across weeks). Project D: Longest deadline, lower urgency. Week 3-4: 200 hours.
solution.schedule: Week 1: Project B (800 hrs). Week 2: Project A (500 hrs) + start C (200 hrs). Week 3: Complete C (100 hrs) + Project D (200 hrs). Total: All projects completed within capacity.
solution.contingency: If B takes longer: A has buffer (could slip to week 2.5). C has most flexibility. Could negotiate D deadline if needed.

exercises.title: Method Selection
exercises.description: Choose appropriate computational methods for industrial problems.
exercises.task:
Match each industrial problem to the most appropriate computational method and explain why:

Problems:
1. Rank 50 kinase inhibitors by binding affinity
2. Predict if a new material will be a semiconductor
3. Understand why a catalyst deactivates
4. Screen 1M compounds for drug-likeness
5. Predict metabolic stability of lead compound

Methods available:
- DFT band structure calculation
- Free Energy Perturbation (FEP)
- Rule-based ADMET filters
- DFT reaction pathway analysis
- Machine learning QSAR

matches.1: FEP - Gold standard for relative binding affinity ranking when compounds are structurally similar. Accuracy ~1 kcal/mol for congeneric series.
matches.2: DFT band structure - Directly calculates electronic structure and band gap to classify as metal/semiconductor/insulator.
matches.3: DFT reaction pathway - Can identify poisoning mechanisms, oxidation states, structural changes that cause deactivation.
matches.4: Rule-based ADMET filters - Fast enough for 1M compounds. ML would also work but rules are interpretable and validated.
matches.5: ML QSAR - Trained on metabolic data, can predict sites of metabolism and clearance rates. Faster than QM for large molecules.

solution.rationale: The key is matching problem requirements (accuracy, speed, interpretability) to method capabilities. FEP for accurate binding, DFT for electronic properties, ML for large-scale predictions, QM for mechanisms.

hints: Match accuracy needs to method capability
